tagged_story_for_tts:
Voices from code. Words without breath.

The term text-to-speech (TTS) does exactly what it says: it turns written words into spoken sound, not by recording a human voice but by building one from scratch—like assembling a puzzle where each piece is a sliver of speech, stretched, pitched, or stitched together.

Early TTS sounded like a robot reading a phonebook. In 1939, Bell Labs’ Voder, the first electronic speech synthesizer, required a trained operator to manually shape sounds with keys and a foot pedal—less like talking, more like playing a clumsy organ. By the 1970s, computers could string together pre-recorded word fragments ("diphones"), but the results were stiff, betrayed by flat intonation and misplaced stresses—imagine a GPS calmly announcing "turn left INTO ONCOMING TRAFFIC."

Modern TTS learns from real voices. Feed a system hours of a single speaker’s recordings, and it detects patterns: how lips purse for "sh", how pitch rises in questions. Algorithms like WaveNet (2016) generate raw audio waveforms sample by sample, mimicking the chaotic physics of human speech—breath, tongue clicks, even hesitations. Today’s best systems can clone a voice from just three seconds of audio, or speak in tones that never existed, like a child’s voice aged backward.

Yet flaws linger. A TTS might butcher "live" (the concert vs. the broadcast), or turn "I saw the saw" into gibberish. The uncanny valley narrows, but never vanishes: a voice too perfect feels hollow, like a smile without teeth.

Three related subjects are voice cloning ethics, speech synthesis history, and how brains process artificial voices.

By the 1970s, computers could string together pre-recorded word fragments ("diphones"), but the results were stiff, betrayed by flat intonation and misplaced stresses—imagine a GPS calmly announcing "turn left INTO ONCOMING TRAFFIC. "

Modern TTS learns from real voices. Feed a system hours of a single speaker’s recordings, and it detects patterns: how lips purse for "sh", how pitch rises in questions. Algorithms like WaveNet (2016) generate raw audio waveforms sample by sample, mimicking the chaotic physics of human speech—breath, tongue clicks, even hesitations.
